---
names: D'Ante Dean,Nicholas Moore, & Sergio Ley
title: "Classification Project"
output: html_document
date: "2026-03-31"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

# Section1.1-.3

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)
library(class)
library(caret)
library(GGally)
library(dplyr)
library(randomForest)
library(vip)
library(gbm)
my_data <- read_csv("baseball.csv")
```

```{r}

# Select a manageable subset of columns
df_small <- my_data %>% select(velocity, spin_rate, pitch_type,release_pos_z,release_pos_x)

# Run GGpairs
ggpairs(df_small)
```

# Section 1.4 | Finding the Best K


```{r}
# Select relevant columns and drop NAs
baseball_knn <- my_data %>%
  select(velocity, spin_rate, release_pos_z, release_pos_x, pitch_type) %>%
  na.omit()

# Standardize numeric features (columns 1â€“4)
baseball_scaled <- baseball_knn
baseball_scaled[, 1:4] <- scale(baseball_scaled[, 1:4])
baseball_scaled <- data.frame(baseball_scaled)
baseball_scaled$pitch_type <- as.factor(baseball_scaled$pitch_type)

# Confirm standardization worked (means ~0, sd ~1)
sapply(baseball_scaled[, 1:4], mean)
sapply(baseball_scaled[, 1:4], sd)
```

```{r}
set.seed(239)
trainIndex <- createDataPartition(baseball_scaled$pitch_type, p = 0.7, list = FALSE)

baseball_train <- baseball_scaled[trainIndex, ]
baseball_test  <- baseball_scaled[-trainIndex, ]

# Verify proportions are similar between sets
prop.table(table(baseball_train$pitch_type))
prop.table(table(baseball_test$pitch_type))
```

```{r}
set.seed(1)
error <- rep(0, 15)

for (i in 1:15) {
  knn_temp <- knn(train = baseball_train[, 1:4],
                  test  = baseball_test[, 1:4],
                  cl    = baseball_train$pitch_type,
                  k     = i)
  error[i] <- 1 - mean(knn_temp == baseball_test$pitch_type)
}

plot_data <- data.frame(k = 1:15, error)
ggplot(data = plot_data, aes(x = k, y = error)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue") +
  geom_point() +
  xlab("Neighborhood Size (k)") +
  ylab("Error Rate") +
  ggtitle("KNN Error Rate by k â€” Baseball Pitch Type")

best_k <- which.min(error)
cat("Best k:", best_k)
```


```{r}
set.seed(1)
knn_best <- knn(train = baseball_train[, 1:4],
                test  = baseball_test[, 1:4],
                cl    = baseball_train$pitch_type,
                k     = best_k)

# Confusion Matrix
cm <- table(Predicted = knn_best, Actual = baseball_test$pitch_type)
cm

# Overall accuracy
accuracy <- mean(knn_best == baseball_test$pitch_type)
cat("Accuracy:", round(accuracy, 4))

# Error rate
cat("Error Rate:", round(1 - accuracy, 4))
```
The model achieved 56.6% accuracy using k = 12. It classifies distinct pitch types well but struggles where velocity and spin rate overlap between classes, suggesting these four features alone have limits in separating every pitch type. \`\`\`

# Section 1.5

```{r}
# =========================
# Section 1.5 | Classification Tree
# =========================

library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)

set.seed(1)

library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)

set.seed(1)

# Train classification tree model
tree_model <- rpart(
  pitch_type ~ velocity + spin_rate + release_pos_z + release_pos_x,
  data = baseball_train,
  method = "class"
)

# Plot the tree
rpart.plot(
  tree_model,
  type = 2,           # label all nodes clearly
  extra = 104,        # shows class + probabilities + % of observations
  fallen.leaves = TRUE,
  box.palette = "RdYlGn",  # color gradient (red â†’ yellow â†’ green)
  shadow.col = "gray",
  nn = TRUE           # show node numbers
)

# Make predictions on test data
tree_pred <- predict(tree_model, baseball_test, type = "class")

# Confusion Matrix
cm_tree <- table(Predicted = tree_pred, Actual = baseball_test$pitch_type)
cm_tree

# Accuracy
tree_accuracy <- mean(tree_pred == baseball_test$pitch_type)
cat("Tree Accuracy:", round(tree_accuracy, 4), "\n")

# Error Rate
cat("Tree Error Rate:", round(1 - tree_accuracy, 4), "\n")

# Complexity table
printcp(tree_model)
```
The classification tree model was used to predict pitch type based on velocity, spin rate, and release position variables. The model creates rule-based splits that make the classification process easy to interpret, with the tree showing how different thresholds separate pitch types.

The model achieved an accuracy of 55.5%, indicating that it captures general patterns in the data but still struggles when pitch characteristics overlap between classes. Compared to KNN, the tree is more interpretative because it clearly shows how decisions are made, but it may be less flexible in handling complex boundaries between pitch types.

#1.6 Bagging
```{r}
### BAG
set.seed(252)
caretBag <- train(pitch_type ~., 
               data = baseball_train, 
               method = "treebag",
               trControl = trainControl("cv", number = 10),
               importance = TRUE
)

predCaretBag <- caretBag %>% predict(baseball_test)

# CONFUSION MATRIX
table(predCaretBag, baseball_test$pitch_type)

# CORRECT RATE
mean(predCaretBag == baseball_test$pitch_type)

## CORRECT RATE
mean(baseball_test$pitch_type == predCaretBag)
vip(caretBag) 
```

# Section 1.6 | Random Forests

```{r}
set.seed(252)

caretRF <- train(pitch_type ~ .,
  data = baseball_train,
  method = "rf",
  trControl = trainControl("cv", number = 10),
  importance = TRUE
)

# Best tuning parameter
caretRF$bestTune

# Final model
caretRF$finalModel

## PREDICT
predCaretRF <- caretRF %>% predict(baseball_test)

## TABLE
table(predCaretRF, baseball_test$pitch_type)

## MEAN
mean(predCaretRF == baseball_test$pitch_type)
```
```{r}
# Plot MeanDecreaseAccuracy
varImpPlot(caretRF$finalModel, type = 1)

# Plot MeanDecreaseGini
varImpPlot(caretRF$finalModel, type = 2)

vip(caretRF$finalModel, type = 1) 
vip(caretRF$finalModel, type = 2) 
```



```{r}
# Section 1.7 | Boosting

set.seed(252)

caretBoost <- train(
  pitch_type ~ .,
  data = baseball_train,
  method = "gbm",
  trControl = trainControl("cv", number = 10),
  verbose = FALSE
)

# Best tuning parameters
caretBoost$bestTune

# Final model
caretBoost$finalModel

# Predict on test data
predCaretBoost <- predict(caretBoost, baseball_test)

# Confusion Matrix
table(Predicted = predCaretBoost, Actual = baseball_test$pitch_type)

# Accuracy
boost_accuracy <- mean(predCaretBoost == baseball_test$pitch_type)
cat("Boosting Accuracy:", round(boost_accuracy, 4), "\n")

# Error Rate
cat("Boosting Error Rate:", round(1 - boost_accuracy, 4), "\n")
```
```{r}
# Variable importance for boosting
vip(caretBoost$finalModel)
varImp(caretBoost)
```


Boosting was used to improve classification by building many small trees sequentially, where each new tree focuses more on the observations that were harder to classify in earlier rounds. This makes boosting different from bagging and random forests, since those methods build trees more independently.

The boosting model was trained using cross-validation and then tested on the holdout test set. Its performance can be evaluated using the confusion matrix, accuracy, and error rate shown above. If the accuracy is higher than the single classification tree, that suggests boosting was able to better capture more complex patterns in pitch type classification.


#5
```{r}
baseball_train_filt <- baseball_train %>%
  filter(pitch_type == 'FF' | pitch_type == 'SL')%>%
  mutate(pitch_type = if_else(pitch_type == "FF", 1, 0))
baseball_test_filt <- baseball_test %>%
  filter(pitch_type == 'FF' | pitch_type == 'SL')%>%
  mutate(pitch_type = if_else(pitch_type == "FF", 1, 0))

modbase <- glm(pitch_type ~ velocity, data = baseball_train_filt, family = "binomial")
summary(modbase)
# logistic
ggplot(data=baseball_train_filt, aes(x=velocity, y=pitch_type))+
  geom_point()+
  geom_line(aes(x = velocity, y = modbase$fitted), color="blue")
```
#6
```{r}
slope<-modbase$coefficients[2]
slope
```

#7
```{r}
pred1<-predict(modbase, newdata = baseball_test_filt)
head(pred1)
```



```{r}
# PREDICT (Response)
pred1R<-predict(modbase, newdata = baseball_test_filt, type="response")
head(pred1R)
# This is the same as using the plogis function on the predicted values of the log odds.
head(plogis(pred1))

# do the prediction for just one person with Glucose of 150
predict(modbase,newdata = data.frame(velocity = 1), type = "response")

# To do the prediction by hand:
modbase$coefficients
1/(exp(-(modbase$coefficients[1]+modbase$coefficients[2]*1))+1) 
```
#8
```{r}
conf_mat1<-data.frame(testOutcome=baseball_test_filt$pitch_type, predOut=pred1R>.5)
table(conf_mat1$predOut, conf_mat1$testOutcome)

## CORRECT RATE
mean(conf_mat1$predOut==conf_mat1$testOutcome)


conf_mat2<-data.frame(testOutcome=baseball_test_filt$pitch_type, predOut=pred1R>.3)
table(conf_mat2$predOut, conf_mat2$testOutcome)

## CORRECT RATE
mean(conf_mat2$predOut==conf_mat2$testOutcome)
```

```{r}
library(GGally)

baseball_train_filt$pitch_type<-as.factor(baseball_train_filt$pitch_type)

ggpairs(baseball_train_filt, 
        ggplot2::aes(color=factor(pitch_type)))

```
#10
```{r}
modMulti<-glm(pitch_type ~.,
          data = baseball_train_filt, family = "binomial")

summary(modMulti)

```
#11
```{r}
library(bestglm)
step(modMulti)
mod0 <- glm(pitch_type ~ 1, family = binomial, data = baseball_train_filt)

## FORWARD Selection
step(mod0, scope = list(upper = modMulti, lower = mod0), 
     method = "forward")
# the Xy matrix needs y as the right-most variable
# trainCaret already has Outcome at the last column
# Also bestglm requires a data.frame, not a tibble. 
best.Xy <- as.data.frame(baseball_train_filt)

# Run best subset
bglm.AIC = bestglm(Xy = best.Xy, family = binomial, IC = "AIC", 
                   TopModels = 10)
bglm.AIC$BestModels

```
#12

```{r}
modBest<-glm(pitch_type ~velocity + spin_rate + release_pos_x,
          data = baseball_train_filt, family = "binomial")

summary(modBest)
```

```{r}

predBest<-predict(modBest, newdata = baseball_test_filt, type="response")
conf_matBest<-data.frame(testOutcome=baseball_test_filt$pitch_type, predOut=predBest>.5)

## Confusion Matrix
table(conf_matBest$predOut, conf_matBest$testOutcome)

## CORRECT RATE
mean(conf_matBest$predOut==conf_matBest$testOutcome)
```


#13
```{r}
"A"
## Confusion Matrix
table(conf_mat1$predOut, conf_mat1$testOutcome)

## CORRECT RATE
mean(conf_mat1$predOut==conf_mat1$testOutcome)

"B"

## Confusion Matrix
table(conf_matBest$predOut, conf_matBest$testOutcome)

## CORRECT RATE
mean(conf_matBest$predOut==conf_matBest$testOutcome)

"C"

## need train, test, cl, and k
trainbase<-baseball_train_filt%>%
  select(-pitch_type)

testbase<-baseball_test_filt%>%
  select(-pitch_type)

trainOut<-baseball_train_filt$pitch_type
testOut<-baseball_test_filt$pitch_type

library(class)

## best pred...
knn.pred12=knn(train = trainbase,
              test = testbase,
              cl = trainOut,
              k=12)

table(knn.pred12,testOut)

## CORRECT RATE
mean(knn.pred12==testOut)

```
